text:
> query language developers can create languages irrespective of the underlying storage system that will ultimately be manipulated by the language.
> processing engine developers can have their processors programmed by any query language for data stored in any storage system. 
> storage systems (such as databases) immediately support any all mm-ADT compliant query languages and processors in support of the multifarious requirements of their end users' data processing requirements.

text:
I'm reminded of Barry Jay's Pattern Calculus.  If on the one hand a hash table is a bunch of key, value pairs and, on the other, a standard lambda takes anything and uses it to come up with something else, you can imagine a pattern as living somewhere between: takes some things and uses them to come up with a value.  Given two patterns you can glue them together.  The standard way is that if the first one matches you take it, otherwise check the second one.

Not sure what he's been up to in the last while <https://scholar.google.com/citations?hl=en&user=eWreFm4AAAAJ&view_op=list_works&sortby=pubdate>.

elements.text:
I'm reminded of Barry Jay's Pattern Calculus.  If on the one hand a hash table is a bunch of key, value pairs and, on the other, a standard lambda takes anything and uses it to come up with something else, you can imagine a pattern as living somewhere between: takes some things and uses them to come up with a value.  Given two patterns you can glue them together.  The standard way is that if the first one matches you take it, otherwise check the second one.

Not sure what he's been up to in the last while

text: <@UA14TGLTC> I looked into Barry Jay’s stuff a while ago and remember not being impressed. Is there something that patterns are better than functions at expressing? One can already compose functions in many different ways.

elements.text: I looked into Barry Jay’s stuff a while ago and remember not being impressed. Is there something that patterns are better than functions at expressing? One can already compose functions in many different ways.

text:
Well, not at all in an important sense, but kind of depending on your domain.  Patterns here basically amount to partial functions.  So if your domain happens to be all about partial functions (that's me sometimes where I end up using a lot of tables), then there's something to find here.  But it's not going to be anything more than having a few functions for nicely composing `a -> Maybe b`, generally.

I guess a potential important difference can be whether you want to model order.  For instance, Rubyist that I am, the keys in a `Hash` are ordered and I use this all the time.  In another context, you may not want to keep that information around.

Another thought that I had while trying to remember the pattern calculus is why prefer a composition operator like this
```<+> :: (a -> Maybe b) -> (a -> Maybe b) -> (a -> Maybe b)
(f <+> g) x = f x `orElse` g x```
over something that collects all the results:
```<*> :: (a -> [b]) -> (a -> [b]) -> (a -> [b])
(f <*> g) x = f x ++ g x```
And so on.

text: I think Jay's most interesting idea was in giving up referential transparency for a kind reflection.  I like being able to look inside my methods.  So that's neat.

text: <@UA14TGLTC> Partial functions are my mortal enemy :innocent:. It’s a strict requirement that my programming language only permits total functions. The challenge is then making it expressive enough that you never feel you “need” a partial function somewhere!

text: Hey Guys, I'm Felipe and I'm from Brazil, but studied and lived in the UK for many years. I just found this community through Ivan Reese's twitter and it's so cool that there other people interested in these kinds of things! I'm always thinkering with new ways of doing things in computing too. Recently what I've been working on is a sort of mechanical programming language called MockMechanics <https://mockmechanics.com/>. With no code you can create musical instruments that actually play, environments that you interact with and even full blown games, for instance I implemented tetris from scratch in a very short time. All these machines I've built can be seen in my channel on youtube <https://www.youtube.com/c/MockMechanics>. And I'm always looking at what else I can do with this system, just yesterday I implemented the bubble sort algorithm, here is a preview <https://mockmechanics.com/mechanics/bubble-sort.mp4> and I'll do a full build video about it soon. Anyway, I just wanted to say hi, and if anyone wants to give MockMechanics a try or talk about it (or other things) I'd be happy to.

attachments.title: MockMechanics
attachments.text: A channel for everything MockMechanics. How the software works, tutorials on how to build specific machines, release notes for new versions, contest announce...

text:
To the best of my knowledge, node-based took off because it replicated the experience of using patch cords with physical equipment (like analog synthesizers, likely many other early analog hand-reprogrammable systems). As those analog systems were rebuilt in the computer, it was somewhat natural to replicate the interface.

Don't know why sheet-based programming didn't similarly take off, but perhaps because there wasn't as much usage of sheet-based physical interfaces for electric systems in industry.

elements.text:

Don't know why sheet-based programming didn't similarly take off, but perhaps because there wasn't as much usage of sheet-based physical interfaces for electric systems in industry.

text: Please do, one thing people were asking for was a video tutorial and I made one, so now anybody can build any of the things I did. I'm also experimenting with a new video format where I show the full build of the machines I make. The bubble sort I talked about will be like this, so just by following the video anybody will be able to recreate the machine and then they can take it on a different direction or use the ideas to create something new entirely.

Hey all, this time we are going to brag a little bit :D: after having good success on reddit, we decided to try putting <https://www.producthunt.com/posts/wasp-lang-alpha|Wasp (Alpha) on Product Hunt> and we managed to end up on the first place yesterday!!
After pushing hard on development during the summer, we are now again balancing between developing the compiler/language and on the other hand building a community, getting first testers, contributors, so getting some exposure on Product Hunt helps with this.
We are done with "launching" Alpha for now, but we do plan to continue reaching out to people by writing hopefully relevant/valuable blog posts.

attachments.title: Wasp-lang Alpha - Develop web apps in React & Nodejs with no boilerplate | Product Hunt
attachments.text: Wasp is an open source configuration language (DSL) for developing, building and deploying full-stack web apps with less boilerplate. It integrates with React, Node.js and Prisma. Wasp is in Alpha right now. Check it out on <https://wasp-lang.dev/>
attachments.fallback: Product Hunt: Wasp-lang Alpha - Develop web apps in React & Nodejs with no boilerplate | Product Hunt
